title: ice_rescue
description: A person has fallen through thin ice on a frozen lake and is struggling to stay afloat.

timeline.title: Person Through Ice
timeline.description: Adult male has broken through ice 30 metres from shore. Air temperature -12°C. Victim conscious but tiring.

timeline.title: Throw Bag Deployed
timeline.description: Rescue throw rope reaches victim. Victim grips rope but cannot self-rescue — arms over ice edge.

timeline.title: Rescue Sled Deployed
timeline.description: Ice rescue sled sent out with rope. Rescuer in ice suit follows on sled to provide contact rescue.

timeline.title: Victim Retrieved
timeline.description: Victim pulled onto sled and dragged to shore. Hypothermia management initiated.

timeline.title: EMS Transport
timeline.description: Victim transported to hospital. Rescue personnel decontaminated and assessed for cold exposure.

node-1.title: Victim Through Ice
node-1.situation: A man has fallen through lake ice approximately 30 metres from shore. Air temperature is -12°C. The ice thickness is estimated at 4–5 cm — unsafe for walking. A bystander is about to walk toward the victim to help.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Stop the bystander, shout instructions to the victim to kick their legs and stay on the ice edge, and immediately retrieve the nearest throw rope or ring buoy.

options.label: B
options.text: Allow the bystander to walk out — they are larger and may support their own weight.

options.label: C
options.text: Walk toward the victim yourself to assess the ice thickness.

node-2.title: Throw Rope Deployed
node-2.situation: A throw rope was tossed and the victim has grabbed it. However, their arms are exhausted and they cannot perform a self-rescue — they are unable to push themselves up onto the ice edge. The rope alone will not extract them.
node-2.question: How do you proceed?

options.label: A
options.text: Deploy the ice rescue sled along the rope line to the victim; assign a trained ice rescue technician in a dry suit and ice pick gloves to follow on their stomach.

options.label: B
options.text: Instruct multiple bystanders to join hands and form a human chain to the victim.

options.label: C
options.text: Drive a rescue boat or snowmobile across the ice to reach the victim faster.

node-3.title: Contact Rescue on Ice
node-3.situation: The ice rescue technician has reached the victim on the sled. The victim is conscious but unable to assist — arms have lost grip strength. The ice surrounding the hole is unstable and cracking as the technician approaches.
node-3.question: What is the safest technique to extract the victim?

options.label: A
options.text: Distribute weight by lying flat on the sled, grab the victim's wrists (not hands), and signal shore crew to haul both rescuer and victim back using the guide rope.

options.label: B
options.text: Pull the victim up onto the ice from a kneeling position to get maximum leverage.

options.label: C
options.text: Have the victim climb onto the rescuer's back to be carried to shore.

node-4.title: Victim at Shore: Hypothermia Management
node-4.situation: The victim is at shore — conscious, severely shivering, slurring words, and unable to stand. Estimated time in water: 12 minutes at -12°C. EMS is 5 minutes away.
node-4.question: What is the correct immediate treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Remove wet clothing carefully, wrap in blankets and a vapour barrier, lay victim horizontally, handle gently to avoid triggering cardiac arrhythmia, and avoid having them walk or stand.

options.label: B
options.text: Vigorously rub the victim's limbs to restore circulation and ask them to jog in place.

node-5.title: Rescuer Cold Exposure
node-5.situation: The ice rescue technician has returned to shore. Despite wearing a dry suit, they report numbness in their fingers and feet. The team wants to continue operations — there may be a second person in the water.
node-5.question: How do you manage the technician?

options.label: A
options.text: Remove the technician from rescue duties, assess them for cold injury, and deploy a second trained technician for continued operations.

options.label: B
options.text: Have the technician continue operations — the numbness is normal and will pass.

node-6.title: Scenario Complete
node-6.situation: The victim was successfully rescued using the reach-throw-sled-contact sequence. Hypothermia management was correctly initiated before EMS arrival. The ice rescue technician was appropriately rotated out of operations. No secondary casualties occurred.
